member.name: Dr Roz Savage

addresses: Watermoor Point, Watermoor Road, Cirencester, GL7 1LF

member.synopsis: Dr Roz Savage is the Liberal Democrat MP for South Cotswolds , and has been an MP continually since 4 July 2024.

officialSourceNotes: Current member identity, party, constituency, thumbnail, contact information, synopsis, written questions, and registered interests are generated from the official UK Parliament Members API.

writtenQuestions.heading: Public Sector: Energy Performance Certificates
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government, how many buildings occupied by the public sector currently require Display Energy Certificates, and of these how many have ratings in each of the defined performance categories b
writtenQuestions.answerText: All buildings occupied by public authorities, with a total useful floor area of 250m2 or greater and frequently visited by the public are required to display a Display Energy Certificate (DEC). It is the building occupier’s responsibility to determine whe...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government

writtenQuestions.heading: Climate Change and Nature
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Energy Security and Net Zero, with reference to the oral contribution of the Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs during the second reading of the Climate and Nature Bill on 24 Ja
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government intends to deliver the second Statement on Climate and Nature to Parliament this summer. Like last year's statement, it will provide an honest appraisal of the state of climate and nature in the UK alongside highlighting the action being ta...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero

writtenQuestions.heading: Doctors: Insurance
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, what his policy is on the professional indemnity arrangements for GPs and hospital consultants in cases arising from Advice and Guidance interactions under the 2026/27 GP contract regulations.
writtenQuestions.answerText: General practitioners (GPs), and other primary care referrers, remain professionally accountable for making appropriate clinical decisions, including referring patients to specialist care when it is in the patient’s best interests. The use of specialist a...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department of Health and Social Care

writtenQuestions.heading: General Practitioners: Contracts
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, whether he plans to publish the safety case underpinning the mandated use of Advice and Guidance in referral pathways prior to the laying of the 2026/27 GP contract regulations.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The contract does not change the clinical threshold for referral to specialist care. General practitioners (GPs) should continue to make a clinical decision to refer for specialist care where that is in the patient’s best interests, and to request special...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department of Health and Social Care

writtenQuestions.heading: General Practitioners: Contracts
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, what assessment he has made of the potential patient safety implications of mandating Advice and Guidance as a required step within GP referral pathways under the 2026/27 GP contract regulations.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The contract does not change the clinical threshold for referral to specialist care. General practitioners (GPs) should continue to make a clinical decision to refer for specialist care where that is in the patient’s best interests, and to request special...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department of Health and Social Care

writtenQuestions.heading: Special Educational Needs

writtenQuestions.answerText: The government’s reform proposals strengthen legal duties that will help better support children with special educational needs and disabilities (SEND) in mainstream settings. All children and young people will have access to an education that meets their...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Education

writtenQuestions.heading: Care Homes: Fees and Charges
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, whether his Department is taking steps to protect self-funders from care home fee increases that exceed inflation.
writtenQuestions.answerText: Tax policy, including changes to National Insurance contributions, is a matter for HM Treasury and assessments of the wider economic impacts are considered in that context.The Department of Health and Social Care does not have the power to set or recommen...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department of Health and Social Care

writtenQuestions.heading: Care Homes: Fees and Charges
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, what assessment she has made of the potential impact of the increase in employers' National Insurance contributions on care home fee rises for self-funders.

writtenQuestions.heading: Special Educational Needs
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Education, whether she plans to publish the evidence, modelling and legal analysis underpinning the Government's position that proposed SEND reforms will strengthen protections for disabled children and young people.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The department published a modelling annex, Child’s Rights Impact Assessment and Equality Impact Assessment alongside the consultation.
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Education

writtenQuestions.heading: Social Services: Finance
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, whether the Government has plans to raise the upper capital limit for means-tested social care support in England.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The capital limits for support with adult social care costs are reviewed annually. The rates for the 2026/27 financial year have been published in the Local Authority Circular, with further information available at the following link:https://www.gov.uk/go...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department of Health and Social Care

writtenQuestions.heading: Voice over Internet Protocol: Rural Areas
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, what assessment the Government has made of the public safety risks associated with the migration from analogue landlines to Digital Voice in rural areas with poor mobile coverage and fr
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government is committed to ensuring that any risks from the industry-led migration of the analogue Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) to digital Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) are mitigated for everyone across the UK, including in rural are...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

writtenQuestions.heading: Agriculture: Fuels
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, what assessment he has made of the potential impact of increased agricultural fuel costs on (a) UK food prices and (b) food security.
writtenQuestions.answerText: It has not proved possible to respond to the hon. Member in the time available before Prorogation.
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs

writtenQuestions.heading: Agriculture: Fuels
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, what discussions he has had with HM Treasury on targeted relief or subsidies for agricultural fuel use.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government is determined to help keep costs down for farmers. Red diesel continues to benefit from an 80% tax discount - saving farmers almost £300 million a year – and continue to provide £11.8 billion to support sustainable farming and food producti...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs

writtenQuestions.heading: Agriculture: Fuels
writtenQuestions.questionText: To ask the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, whether her Department plans to introduce any short-term financial support for farmers to mitigate rising fuel costs in 2026.
writtenQuestions.answerText: The Government is conscious of the increases to prices of red diesel and other fuel influenced by the continued instability in the Middle East. The Competition and Markets Authority has been asked to look into industry concerns about red diesel prices, in...
writtenQuestions.answeringBody: Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs

Name of donor: BOS-UK Borneo Orangutan Survival UK Address of donor: Borneo Orangutan Survival UK, 483 Green Lanes, London N13 4BS Estimate of the probable value (or amount of any donation): £3,152.85 return flights from London (£2,218.13), internal flight from Jakarta to Palangkaraya (£81.70), internal flight from Palangkaraya to Jakarta (£93.25), internal transport (£124.00), accommodation in Jakarta (£41.06), accommodation in Palangaraya (£205.23), medical tests (£340.00), e-Visa (£23.65) and buffet dinners (£25.84) Destination of visit: Indonesia Dates of visit: 26 March 2026 to 10 April 2026 Purpose of visit: ICCF (International Climate Conservation Fund) and several members of the International Conservation APPG went. The purpose was to learn about the issues surrounding deforestation in Indonesia through a collaborative field mission with a delegation of legislators and staff. (Registered 5 June 2026)
